coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of green-photoluminescent single layer graphene quantum dots: Determination of HOMO and LUMO energy states
coredata.dc:creator: Bayat, A.
Saievar-Iranizad, E.
coredata.dc:description: Low-cost and high-yield green-photoluminescent single layer graphene quantum dots (SLGQDs) synthesized with only DI water and glucose as precursor using a hydrothermal method. Atomic force microscopy proved that the graphene quantum dots are single layers and transmission electron microscopy images showed that the average width of SLGQDs is about 8nm and optical characterization revealed that SLGQDs are highly photoluminescent. Highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the SLGQDs against vacuum determined using a cyclic voltammetry technique, and energy band gap was obtained ∼ 2.9 and 3.1eV from cyclic voltammetry and optical measurements, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Single layer graphene quantum dots
Cyclic voltammetry
Band gap
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of HOMO and LUMO energy states

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation mechanism of the SLGQDs via a hydrothermal method at 200°C for 8h.

(a) TEM image of the SLGQDs and the inset shows their HRTEM image, (b) AFM image of the mica substrate and the inset shows the height profile of mica substrate (line1), (c) AFM image of the SLGQDs on the mica substrate and (d) height profile of the SLGQDs (line2).
(a) FTIR and (b) Raman spectra of the SLGQDs.
UV–Vis absorption spectrum, PL spectra and Tauc plot of the SLGQDs. PL spectra excited at the wavelengths from 450 to 520nm and Photographs of the SLGQDs solution are under UV (365nm) and daylight.
(a) Cyclic voltammetry measurements of the SLGQDs/Glassy carbon and bare Glassy carbon electrodes, (b) Positions of HOMO and LUMO energy states of the SLGQDs against vacuum and Ag/AgCl.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of green-photoluminescent single layer graphene quantum dots: Determination of HOMO and LUMO energy states

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O.Box: 14115-111, Tehran, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low-cost and high-yield green-photoluminescent single layer graphene quantum dots (SLGQDs) synthesized with only DI water and glucose as precursor using a hydrothermal method. Atomic force microscopy proved that the graphene quantum dots are single layers and transmission electron microscopy images showed that the average width of SLGQDs is about 8nm and optical characterization revealed that SLGQDs are highly photoluminescent. Highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the SLGQDs against vacuum determined using a cyclic voltammetry technique, and energy band gap was obtained ∼ 2.9 and 3.1eV from cyclic voltammetry and optical measurements, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms in two-dimensional (2D) lattice, has extraordinary mechanical, thermal, electronic and chemical properties [1–5]. Graphene has no optical luminescence because of its zero band gap energy [6]. To effectively tune the band gap of graphene, a promising approach is to minify graphene sheets into graphene quantum dots (GQDs, typically smaller than 30nm) [7]. Due to their quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs have unique properties such as an ultraviolet-blue to green luminescence, an excellent photostability, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, being environmentally benign, cost effective preparation methods. GQDs are a good candidate for a wide range of applications such as photocatalysis, sensing, light emitting diodes (LEDs), and energy conversion or energy storage devices and can replace traditional QDs [8–12].

So far, various methods have been utilized to prepare GQDs, including opening fullerene cage [13], mechanical grinding exfoliation [14], electrochemical approach [7], electron-beam lithography [15], chemical oxidation of CX-72 carbon black [16], cutting large graphene oxide sheets via a hydrothermal route [17] and carbonizing suitable organic precursors [18]. However, most of the above-mentioned methods suffer from several disadvantages such as the difficulty in obtaining their starting materials, difficulty in functionalizing, being expensive, low-yield and their poor optoelectrical properties. For example, the methods of opening the fullerene cage on ruthenium surfaces and carving graphite crystallites by high-resolution electron-beam lithography are limited by their low productivity and high cost. However, preparing of single-layer graphene quantum dots (SLGQDs) using inexpensive, simple methods in a large quantity is one of the biggest obstacles in field of graphene research. The preparing SLGQDs with a uniform size distribution using the above-mentioned methods is very difficult, and only some of the methods have succeeded. Dong et al. reported synthesis of SLGQDs using chemical oxidation of CX-72 carbon black [16]. Chi et al. reported synthesis of SLGQDs using hydrothermal etching of single wall carbon nanotube [19] and Jiang, et al. reported synthesis of single- and double-layer GQDs using H2O2 treatment of graphene oxide [20]. Thus, there is a great need to develop a more efficient, more facile and less expensive preparation method to synthesize SLGQDs in a large scale.

Here, a low-cost, high-yield, one-step, and facile process was reported to prepare SLGQDs from only glucose powder as precursor in DI water, and their highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were determined directly using a cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique.

In a typical synthesis, 0.05g of glucose powder was dissolved in 50ml of deionized water. The mixture treated by a one-step hydrothermal method in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (75ml) at 200°C for 8h. The initial liquid sample was transparent (colorless) and it changed to orange after synthesis of SLGQDs.

The height profile of the obtained SLGQDs was characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Veeco Autoprobe CP research) in contact mode on mica sheets as substrates. Photoluminescence (PL) emission was recorded using PL spectroscopy (Gilden photonics) and optical absorbance measurements were carried out using a spectrophotometer (Unico 4802). Structural morphology was studied using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, CM30 300kV). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using a FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet Nexus 670). Raman analysis (BRUKER, Germany, SENTERRA) was used to prove the formation of graphene structure. CV measurements were performed with a three electrode configuration, i.e. SLGQDs/glassy carbon as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and a platinum wire as the counter electrode in an acetonitrile solution including 0.1M Tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6).

Under the hydrothermal process, glucose molecules are dehydrated to form C=C, which is the elementary unit of the graphene structure. During the formation of SLGQDs, hydrogen atoms of a glucose molecule interact with hydroxyl groups of an adjacent glucose molecule leading to formation of water molecules; consequently, carbon atoms covalently interact with each other and finally graphene quantum dots are formed, as schematically shown in Fig. 1.

Fig. 2-a shows the TEM image of the obtained SLGQDs. As can be observed, they have a uniform dispersion without any apparent aggregation with an average size of about 8nm and the inset in Fig. 2-a displays the typical HRTEM image of the as-prepared SLGQDs, showing the lattice parameter of 0.37nm.

Fig. 2-b shows the AFM image of the mica surface. As can be seen, the surface of mica is very smooth and the measurement error in the height profile measurements of the AFM analysis is 0.1nm. Thus, SLGQDs with a thickness of about ∼0.35nm [21] can be determined on the mica substrate. AFM image of the SLGQDs in Fig. 2-c and height profile in the Fig. 2-d show that the topographic height of the SLGQDs is less than 0.5nm, suggesting that the GQDs are single-layered.

FTIR spectroscopy was applied to characterize the functional groups of the prepared SLGQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 3-a, the following peaks are observed: hydroxyl groups including a broad peak at 3400cm−1 related to the O-H stretching vibration and at 1290cm−1 related to the O-H bending vibrations in C-OH, and this functional group improves the hydrophilicity and stability of SLGQDs in aqueous systems. The peak at 1620cm−1 is related to the C˭C stretching vibrations, the peak at 1730cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of C˭O and the peak at 1380cm−1 is related to the symmetric stretching vibration of carboxylic group –COO. All peaks in this figure are in agreement with the peaks of GQDs [17,22,23].

Raman spectroscopy was employed to characterize the graphene structure. Graphite has two peaks at about 1350 and 1580cm−1 that can be assigned to the disordered sp2 carbon (D-band) and well-ordered graphite (G-band) [21,24], respectively. The intensity ratio of D to G bands indicates a disorder in graphite, which originates from defects associated with grain boundaries, vacancies and amorphous carbons. Raman spectrum of the SLGQDs is shown in Fig. 3-b. Two peaks are observed at about 1340 and 1615cm−1 in the Raman spectrum for the SLGQDs. It is well understood that when graphite powder is exfoliated to its graphene sheets, G band shifts to higher values. On the other hand, cutting graphene sheets into quantum dots leads to widening of the G band. These two behaviors were observed for the SLGQDs and proved that a quantum confinement is happened. It should be noted that the D and G bands of the SLGQDs are not very sharp due to a strong background signal, and in order to observe the D and G bands more clear, the intensity of the spectrum was amplified.

UV–vis absorption spectrum of the SLGQDs solution is shown in Fig. 4. The primary peak at 230nm was assigned to π–π* transitions of aromatic C=C bonds in sp2 hybridization region and peak at about 300nm is attributed to the n–π* transition of C=O bonds [25,26]. Thus, the absorption spectrum could also provide the fingerprints of GQDs structures. According to this figure, absorption region is recorded at all wavelengths in the spectrum and a strong optical absorption appeared near the UV region with a tail extending in the visible range. Such wide absorption allows excitation with a spectrally wide light source, necessary to perform efficiently in photovoltaic devices. It is well known that the band gap caused by quantum confinement effects (QCE) [27,28] depends on size, that is, the smaller the size, the bigger the energy gap. Accordingly, the strong quantum confinement effect leads to the enhanced band gap opening. By plotting the square of the absorption energy (αhv, where α is the absorbance and hv is the photon energy) against hv (known as Tauc plots [29]), the direct band-gap energy is determined as 3.1eV (inset in Fig. 4).

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp and the SLGQDs solution was excited at wavelengths from 450 to 520nm with an interval of 10nm. The corresponding PL spectra are shown in Fig. 4. The emission peaks of the sample at various excitation wavelengths did not shift and the maximum emission wavelength remained at around 540nm, which implies that both the size and the surface state of the sample remain uniform and the product is green-photoluminescent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of HOMO and LUMO energy states
ce:section.ce:para:
Cyclic voltammetry measurements were utilized to directly determine the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of the SLGQDs, and hence to find the bandgap energy. Applying potentials above the LUMO to form an accumulation layer, or below the HOMO to form inversion layers, can lead to abrupt emergence of cathodic and anodic currents, respectively [30–33]. Electrode for electrochemical analysis was prepared by drop-casting SLGQDs suspensions (5μl) and nafion (0.5μl, 5% w) onto a glassy carbon electrode (diameter of 3mm) and dried naturally at room temperature. Fig. 5-a shows the behavior of the glassy carbon electrode in the absence and presence of the SLGQDs. The oxidation and reduction peaks related to electron and hole injections into the conduction and valence bands were consequently utilized to determine the EHOMO and ELUMO using the following three equations [34]
E
HOMO
= − e
(
E
Ox , vs .
NHE
+ 4.75
)
(
eV
)
E
LUMO
= − e
(
E
Red , vs .
NHE
+ 4.75
)
( eV )
E
NHE
=
E
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.197

The SLGQDs/glassy carbon electrode shows a reduction peak at −0.7V and an oxidation peak at +2.2V versus Ag/AgCl. The values for ELUMO and EHOMO calculated from these peaks are ELUMO= −4.25eV and EHOMO= −7.15eV versus vacuum, respectively. The band-gap energy determined from the cyclic voltammetry was 2.9eV, which is in a good agreement with that obtained from the optical absorption spectrum (3.1eV).

Green-photoluminescent single-layer graphene quantum dots were obtained using only glucose powder and DI water as precursors via a hydrothermal method. This facile method shows the prominent advantages of low-cost, high-yield and large-scale production over the reported ones. LUMO and HOMO energy states were determined against vacuum using a cyclic voltammetry technique. Because of the unique properties of the obtained single-layer graphene quantum dots, the product can be used for advanced technologies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Research Council of the Tarbiat Modares University for financial supports.
